sections.heading: What is JPA?
Relationship types
sections.content:
// @OneToMany — delivery has many stops
@OneToMany(mappedBy = "delivery", fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
private List<Stop> stops;

// @ManyToOne — stop belongs to one delivery
@ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
@JoinColumn(name = "delivery_id")
private Delivery delivery;

// @ManyToMany — drivers have many skills, skills have many drivers
@ManyToMany
@JoinTable(name = "driver_skill",
    joinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "driver_id"),
    inverseJoinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "skill_id"))
private Set<Skill> skills;
GOLDEN RULE: Always use FetchType.LAZY `FetchType.EAGER` loads related entities EVERY time you load the parent. With 100 deliveries, each with 10 stops → 1000 stops loaded even if you only needed the delivery status. `FetchType.LAZY` loads related entities only when you ACCESS them. Use JOIN FETCH when you actually need them.

sections.heading: The N+1 problem and solutions
sections.content:
// ❌ N+1: loads 100 deliveries, then 1 query per delivery for stops
List<Delivery> deliveries = repo.findAll(); // 1 query
for (Delivery d : deliveries) {
    d.getStops().size(); // 100 queries! (one per delivery)
}

// ✅ JOIN FETCH — one query loads everything
@Query("SELECT d FROM Delivery d JOIN FETCH d.stops WHERE d.status = :status")
List<Delivery> findWithStops(@Param("status") DeliveryStatus status);
// 1 query with JOIN — all stops included

// ✅ @EntityGraph — declarative
@EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"stops", "driver"})
List<Delivery> findByStatus(DeliveryStatus status);

// ✅ @BatchSize — reduces N+1 to N/batch+1
@BatchSize(size = 25)
@OneToMany(mappedBy = "delivery")
private List<Stop> stops;
// Loads stops in batches of 25 (4 queries for 100 deliveries instead of 100)
Detection: Enable `spring.jpa.show-sql=true` in dev. If you see the same query repeated with different IDs → N+1.

sections.heading: Query approaches compared
sections.content:
// 1. JPQL — object-oriented SQL (portable, type-safe-ish)
@Query("SELECT d FROM Delivery d WHERE d.status = :status AND d.scheduledAt > :after")
List<Delivery> findPlanned(@Param("status") DeliveryStatus s, @Param("after") LocalDateTime t);

// 2. Criteria API — programmatic, fully type-safe (verbose)
CriteriaBuilder cb = em.getCriteriaBuilder();
CriteriaQuery<Delivery> q = cb.createQuery(Delivery.class);
Root<Delivery> d = q.from(Delivery.class);
q.where(cb.equal(d.get("status"), status),
        cb.greaterThan(d.get("scheduledAt"), after));
// Good for dynamic filters (optional parameters)

// 3. Native SQL — when you need DB-specific features
@Query(value = """
    SELECT d.* FROM logistics.delivery d
    WHERE d.status = :status
    AND d.scheduled_at > :after
    ORDER BY d.scheduled_at
    LIMIT :limit
    """, nativeQuery = true)
List<Delivery> findPlannedNative(...);
When to use each: • JPQL: standard queries, portable, works with entity names • Criteria: dynamic filters (search forms with optional fields) • Native: PostgreSQL-specific (JSONB, DISTINCT ON, window functions, CTEs) Key rule: NEVER `SELECT *` in native queries for performance-critical paths. Project only needed columns into DTOs.
